text: If a unit positive charge is taken from one point to another over an equipotential surface, then
options: work is done on the charge
work is done by the charge
explanation:
Potential difference between any two points on equipotential surface is zero.

i.e., \( V_1 - V_2 = 0 \)

Work done to move a unit positive charge from one point to another,

\( W = q(V_1 - V_2) = q[0] = 0 \).
name: Questions Item 1

text: The variation of electric potential with distance from a fixed point is shown in figure. What is the value of electric field at \( x = 2 \) m?
explanation:
According to given diagram, potential is constant in region AB.

i.e., \( V = \text{constant} \)

Electric field at \( x = 2 \) m (at point C),

\( E = -\frac{dV}{dx} = 0 \quad [\because V = \text{constant}] \).
name: Questions Item 2

text: Three capacitors of capacitances \( C_1 = 2 \mu\text{F} \), \( C_2 = 3 \mu\text{F} \) and \( C_3 = 5 \mu\text{F} \) are connected in series. A potential difference of 155 V is applied across the combination. Choose the correct option.
options: Least potential difference is across \( C_3 \). Equivalent capacitance of combination is \( \frac{30}{31} \mu\text{F} \). The voltage across \( C_1 \) is 75 V.
Least potential difference is across \( C_1 \). Equivalent capacitance of combination is \( \frac{30}{51} \mu\text{F} \). The voltage across \( C_2 \) is 50 V.
Least potential difference is across \( C_1 \). Equivalent capacitance of combination is \( \frac{30}{31} \mu\text{F} \). The voltage across \( C_3 \) is 30 V.
Least potential difference is across \( C_2 \). Equivalent capacitance of combination is \( \frac{30}{31} \mu\text{F} \). The voltage across \( C_1 \) is 50 V.
explanation:
Equivalent capacitance is given as

\( \frac{1}{C} = \frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{3} + \frac{1}{5} = \frac{30 + 20 + 12}{30} \)

\( \Rightarrow C = \frac{30}{31} \mu\text{F} \)

In series combination of capacitors, charge flowing through each capacitor is same which is given by

\( q = CV = \frac{30}{31} \times 155 = 150 \mu\text{C} \)

Potential difference across \( C_1 \),

\( V_1 = \frac{q}{C_1} = \frac{150}{2} = 75 \text{V} \)

Potential difference across \( C_2 \),

\( V_2 = \frac{150}{3} = 50 \text{V} \)

Potential difference across \( C_3 \),

\( V_3 = \frac{150}{5} = 30 \text{V} \)

Hence, potential difference across \( C_3 \) is least as 30 V.
name: Questions Item 3

text: The electric potential at the surface of a charged spherical conductor of radius 5 cm is 200 V. The work done in moving a charge of +5 C from the surface to a point 10 cm from the centre of the sphere is
explanation:
Electric potential on the surface of sphere,

\( V = 200 \text{V} \)

Radius, \( R = 5 \text{cm} = 5 \times 10^{-2} \text{m} \)

Charge, \( q = 5 \text{C} \)

Electric potential on the surface of sphere,

\( V = 9 \times 10^9 \frac{Q}{R} \)

\( 200 = 9 \times 10^9 \frac{Q}{5 \times 10^{-2}} \)

\( Q = \frac{200 \times 5 \times 10^{-2}}{9 \times 10^9} \)

Work done to move the charge from point A to point B is given as

\( W = V_A - V_B \)

\( = \left( \frac{9 \times 10^9 Q}{5 \times 10^{-2}} - \frac{9 \times 10^9 Q}{10 \times 10^{-2}} \right) \times 5 \)

\( = 45 \times 10^9 \times \frac{Q}{10} \left( \frac{1}{5} - \frac{1}{10} \right) \times 5 \)

\( = 450 \times 10^9 \times \frac{Q}{50} \times 5 \)

\( = 45 \times 10^9 \times \frac{5}{50} \times Q \times 5 \)

From Eq. (i), we get

\( W = 166.66 \text{J} \approx 166.7 \text{J} \).
name: Questions Item 4

text: The capacitance of a spherical condenser is 1 μF. If the spacing between the two spheres is 1 mm, the radius of the outer sphere is
explanation:
Given, capacitance of spherical capacitor,

\( C = 1 \mu\text{F} = 10^{-6} \text{F} \)

Spacing between two spheres, \( d = 1 \text{mm} = 10^{-3} \text{m} \)

Capacitance of spherical capacitor,

\( C = 4 \pi \epsilon_0 \frac{r_1 r_2}{r_2 - r_1} \)

\( \Rightarrow 10^7 = \frac{1}{1} + \frac{1}{r_2} \quad (\because r_1 = 1 \text{m} \text{ and } r_2 \gg r_1) \)

\( \Rightarrow r_2 = 10^7 \text{m} \approx 10 \text{m} \).
name: Questions Item 5

text: A 100 V battery is connected across the series combination of the two capacitors of 4 μF and 8 μF. The energy stored in the series combination is
explanation:
Potential difference across terminals of battery, \( V = 100 \text{V} \)

Equivalent capacitance of series combination is given as

\( \frac{1}{C} = \frac{1}{4} + \frac{1}{8} = \frac{3}{8} \)

\( \Rightarrow C = \frac{8}{3} \mu\text{F} \)

Same charge will flow in series combination which is given as

\( q = CV = \frac{8}{3} \times 10^{-6} \times 100 = \frac{8}{3} \times 10^{-4} \text{C} \)

Energy stored,

\( E = \frac{1}{2} \frac{q^2}{C} = \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{\left( \frac{8}{3} \times 10^{-4} \right)^2}{\frac{8}{3} \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{4}{3} \times 10^{-2} \text{J} \)

\( \Rightarrow E = 1.33 \times 10^{-2} \text{J} \).
name: Questions Item 6

text: When a capacitor is connected to a battery
options: an alternating current flow in the circuit
no current flow in the circuit
a current flow for sometime and finally it decreases to zero
current keeps on increasing and reaches maximum after sometime
explanation: When a capacitor is connected to a battery (DC source), then it is impossible to flow a steady current but a transient current flows through the capacitor plates, i.e. current flows for sometime and finally it decreases to zero.
name: Questions Item 7

text: Taking earth to be a metallic sphere, its capacity will approximately be
explanation:
Radius of earth, \( R = 6400 \text{km} = 6.4 \times 10^6 \text{m} \)

Taking earth to be a metallic sphere, its capacitance is given as

\( C = 4 \pi \epsilon_0 R = \frac{1}{9 \times 10^9} \times 6.4 \times 10^6 \)

\( = 0.7111 \times 10^{-3} \text{F} = 711.1 \times 10^{-6} \text{F} \)

\( = 711 \mu\text{F} \approx 700 \mu\text{F} \).
name: Questions Item 8

text: Electric potential at a point distant 0.5 m from a spherical conductor of radius 0.2 m charged to +1 nC is
explanation:
Charge on spherical conductor, \( q = +1 \text{nC} = 10^{-9} \text{C} \)

Radius, \( R = 0.2 \text{m} \)

Distance of point from spherical conductor, \( r = 0.5 \text{m} \)

Electric potential,

\( V = \frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_0} \cdot \frac{q}{r} = 9 \times 10^9 \times \frac{10^{-9}}{0.5} = \frac{9}{0.5} = 18 \text{V} \).
name: Questions Item 9

text: When a 5 C charge is kept in a uniform electric field, a force of 5000 N acts on it. Find the potential difference between two points in that field, separated by a distance of 1 cm.
explanation:
Charge, \( q = 5 \text{C} \)

Force, \( F = 5000 \text{N} \), \( d = 1 \text{cm} = 10^{-2} \text{m} \)

Potential difference between the given points,

\( V = E \times d = \frac{F}{q} \times d \quad \left[ \because E = \frac{F}{q} \right] \)

\( = \frac{5000}{5} \times 10^{-2} = 10 \text{V} \).
name: Questions Item 10

text: The electric potential at the surface of an atomic nucleus (Z = 50) of radius \( 9 \times 10^{-15} \) m is
explanation:
Atomic number, \( Z = 50 \)

Radius, \( r = 9 \times 10^{-15} \) m

Electric potential at the surface of atomic nucleus,

\( V = \frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_0} \cdot \frac{q}{r} = \frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_0} \cdot \frac{Ze}{r} \quad [\because q = Ze] \)

\( = 9 \times 10^9 \times \frac{50 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}{9 \times 10^{-15}} = 8 \times 10^6 \) V.
name: Questions Item 11

text: The capacity of parallel plate condenser is 5 μF. When a glass plate is placed between the plates of the condenser, its potential difference reduces to 1/8 of the original value. The magnitude of relative dielectric constant of glass is
explanation:
Given, \( C = 5 \mu\text{F} = 5 \times 10^{-6} \) F

Initial potential = V

When glass plate of dielectric constant K is introduced between the plates of capacitor, then potential difference is

\( V' = \frac{V}{8} \)

\( \therefore K = \frac{V}{V'} = \frac{V}{V/8} \)

\( \Rightarrow K = 8 \).
name: Questions Item 12

text: When a charge of 20 C is taken from one point to another separated by a distance of 0.2 m, work of 2 J is required to be done. What is the potential difference between the two points?
explanation:
Charge, \( q = 20 \text{C} \)

\( d = 0.2 \text{m} \)

\( W = 2 \text{J} \)

Potential difference,

\( V = \frac{W}{q} = \frac{2}{20} = 0.1 \text{V} \).
name: Questions Item 13

text: The electric potential at a point on the axis of an electric dipole depends on the distance r of the point from the dipole as
explanation:
Electric potential at point on the axial position of electric dipole is given as

\( V = \frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_0} \cdot \frac{p}{r^2} \)

i.e.,

\( V \propto \frac{1}{r^2} \Rightarrow V \propto r^{-2} \).
name: Questions Item 14

text: When two identical capacitors are charged individually to different potentials and then connected in parallel, after disconnecting from the source
options: net charge = sum of initial charges
net potential difference ≠ sum of individual initial potential difference
net energy stored < sum of individual initial energy
explanation:
Let say capacitance of two identical capacitors be C and they are charged through a potential \( V_1 \) and \( V_2 \), respectively.

After connecting in parallel,

Net potential difference,

\( V_c = \frac{C V_1 + C V_2}{C + C} = \frac{V_1 + V_2}{2} \)

Which is not equal to the sum of individual initial potential difference, i.e. \( V_c \neq V_1 + V_2 \).

For an isolated system, charge remains always conserved,

i.e. sum of initial charge = sum of final charge.

Since, potential difference across each capacitors are different and connected in parallel, so charge redistribution will take place from higher potential capacitor to lower potential capacitor. As we know that if charge flow, then there is some loss of energy in form of heat.

i.e., \( U_f < U_i \).
name: Questions Item 15

text: An imaginary equilateral triangle ABC of side length 2 m is placed in a uniform electric field E = 10 NC⁻¹ as shown. Then, \( V_A - V_B \)
explanation:
As we know, \( V_A - V_B = -\int_A^B E \cdot dr \)

Here, E is constant. So, we may write above expression as

\( V_A - V_B = -E \cdot r \cos \theta \)

where, r is distance between points A and B, θ is angle between E and displacement vector r from point A to point B.

\( \therefore V_A - V_B = -10 \times 2 \times \cos 60^\circ \ (\theta = 60^\circ \ \text{from figure}) \)

\( = -10 \times 2 \times \frac{1}{2} = -10 \text{V} \).
name: Questions Item 16

text: A capacitor is made of a flat plate of area A and a second plate of stair-like structure as shown in the figure. The area of each stair is \( \frac{A}{3} \) and the height is d. The capacitance of the arrangement is
explanation:
Given, area of each stair = \( \frac{A}{3} \)

and height of each stair = d

Capacitance of parallel plate capacitor is given as

\( C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d} \)

First capacitance of capacitor,

\( C_1 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{3d} \)

second capacitance of capacitor,

\( C_2 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{3(2d)} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{6d} \)

and third capacitance of capacitor,

\( C_3 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{3(3d)} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{9d} \)

Equivalent capacitance of capacitor,

\( C_{\text{eq}} = C_1 + C_2 + C_3 \)

\( C_{\text{eq}} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{3d} + \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{6d} + \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{9d} \)

\( C_{\text{eq}} = \frac{(6 \varepsilon_0 + 3 \varepsilon_0 + 2 \varepsilon_0)A}{18d} = \frac{11 \varepsilon_0 A}{18d} \).
name: Questions Item 17

text: The radius of a soap bubble is r and the surface tension of the soap solution is S. The electric potential to which the soap bubble be raised by charging it so that the pressure inside the bubble becomes equal to the pressure outside the bubble is (ε₀ = permittivity of the free space)
explanation:
Pressure due to surface tension inside the soap bubble \( p_i = \frac{4S}{r} \)

Electrostatic pressure outside the soap bubble, \( p_0 = \frac{\sigma^2}{2\epsilon_0} \)

where, \( \sigma \) = surface charge density and \( \epsilon_0 \) = permittivity of the free space

Electric potential, \( V = \frac{kQ}{r} \)

\( \Rightarrow V = \frac{k(\sigma A)}{r} \ (\because Q = \sigma.A) \)

Area of sphere, \( A = 4\pi r^2 \)

So, \( V = \frac{1}{4\pi\epsilon_0}\left(\frac{\sigma 4\pi r^2}{r}\right) = \frac{\sigma r}{\epsilon_0} \ (\because k = \frac{1}{4\pi\epsilon_0}) \)

or \( \sigma = \frac{\epsilon_0 V}{r} \)

According to the question, \( p_i = p_0 \)

\( \frac{4S}{r} = \frac{\sigma^2}{2\epsilon_0} \Rightarrow \frac{4S}{r} = \frac{\left(\frac{\epsilon_0 V}{r}\right)^2}{2\epsilon_0} \Rightarrow V = \sqrt{\frac{8Sr}{\epsilon_0}} \).
name: Questions Item 18

text: The potential difference (V_A - V_B) in the arrangement shown in the figure is (q = 1 μC, x = 2 cm, y = 3 cm)
explanation:
An arrangement is shown in the figure.

Here, potential at a point A,

\( V_A = V_{A^{+q}} + V_{A^{-q}} \)

\( = \frac{9 \times 10^9 \times 10^{-6}}{2 \times 10^{-2}} - \frac{9 \times 10^9 \times 10^{-6}}{5 \times 10^{-2}} = 9 \times 10^5 \left[ \frac{1}{2} - \frac{1}{5} \right] \)

Similarly, potential at a point B,

\( V_B = V_{B^{+q}} + V_{B^{-q}} = 9 \times 10^5 \left[ \frac{1}{5} - \frac{1}{2} \right] \)

Hence, \( V_A - V_B = 9 \times 10^5 \left[ \frac{1}{2} - \frac{1}{5} - \frac{1}{5} + \frac{1}{2} \right] \)

\( \Rightarrow V_A - V_B = 54 \times 10^5 \text{V} \).
name: Questions Item 19

text: Three point charges of 3 μC, 4 μC, and 5 μC are arranged at the three corners of a right angled triangle ABC as shown in the figure. The work done in moving the charges at A and C, so that the three charges are located at the three corners of an equilateral triangle of side 3 cm is
explanation:
In a right angle triangle, in ΔABC

\( AC^2 = AB^2 + BC^2 \)

\( \Rightarrow AC = \sqrt{AB^2 + BC^2} = \sqrt{(4 \times 10^{-2})^2 + (3 \times 10^{-2})^2} \)

\( \Rightarrow AC = 5 \times 10^{-2} \text{m} \)

Initial electric potential energy of three charges,

\( U = \frac{kq_1 q_2}{AB} + \frac{kq_1 q_3}{AC} + \frac{kq_2 q_3}{BC} \)

\( = \frac{9 \times 10^9 \times 3 \times 10^{-6} \times 4 \times 10^{-6}}{0.03} + \frac{9 \times 10^9 \times 3 \times 10^{-6} \times 5 \times 10^{-6}}{0.05} + \frac{9 \times 10^9 \times 4 \times 10^{-6} \times 5 \times 10^{-6}}{0.04} \)

\( = 3 \times 10^9 \times (12 \times 10^{-12} + 15 \times 10^{-12} + 20 \times 10^{-12}) \)

\( = 3 \times 10^9 \times 47 \times 10^{-12} = 141.1 \times 10^{-3} \text{J} \)

Hence, the work done in moving charges at points A and C,

\( W = U' - U = 0.444 - 0.141 = 0.303 \text{J} \).
name: Questions Item 20

text: The potential difference (V_A - V_B) in the arrangement shown in the figure is (q = 1 μC, x = 2 cm, y = 3 cm)
explanation:
An arrangement is shown in the figure.

Here, potential at a point A,

\( V_A = V_{A^{+q}} + V_{A^{-q}} \)

\( = \frac{9 \times 10^9 \times 10^{-6}}{2 \times 10^{-2}} - \frac{9 \times 10^9 \times 10^{-6}}{5 \times 10^{-2}} = 9 \times 10^5 \left[ \frac{1}{2} - \frac{1}{5} \right] \)

Similarly, potential at a point B,

\( V_B = V_{B^{+q}} + V_{B^{-q}} = 9 \times 10^5 \left[ \frac{1}{5} - \frac{1}{2} \right] \)

Hence, \( V_A - V_B = 9 \times 10^5 \left[ \frac{1}{2} - \frac{1}{5} - \frac{1}{5} + \frac{1}{2} \right] \)

\( \Rightarrow V_A - V_B = 54 \times 10^5 \text{V} \).
name: Questions Item 21

text: In a parallel plate capacitor the separation between plates is 3x. This separation is filled by two layers of dielectrics, in which one layer has thickness x and dielectric constant 3k, the other layer is of thickness 2x and dielectric constant 5k. If the plates of the capacitor are connected to a battery, then the ratio of potential difference across the dielectric layers is
explanation:
Key Idea Capacitance of a parallel plate capacitor is given by relation, \( C = \varepsilon_r \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d} \) where, \( \varepsilon_r \) = dielectric constant, \( A \) = area of parallel plate and \( d \) = distance between the plate.

Here, \( d_0 = 3x \), \( d_1 = x \), \( \varepsilon_1 = 3k \), \( d_2 = 2x \) and \( \varepsilon_2 = 5k \)

The capacitor is shown in the figure below,

Now, \( C_1 = 3k \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{x} \) and \( C_2 = 5k \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{2x} \)

Since, m in series combination of capacitor, stored charge in each capacitor is same.

So, \( V_1 = \frac{Q}{C_1} \) and \( V_2 = \frac{Q}{C_2} \)

\( \Rightarrow V_1 = \frac{\theta x}{3k \varepsilon_0 A} \) and \( V_2 = \frac{\theta 2x}{5k \varepsilon_0 A} \)

The ratio, \( \frac{V_1}{V_2} = \frac{3k \varepsilon_0 A}{\theta x} \cdot \frac{\theta 2x}{5k \varepsilon_0 A} = \frac{5}{3 \times 2} = \frac{5}{6} \).
name: Questions Item 22

text: Three point charges of 2 mC each are kept at the vertices of an equilateral triangle of side 50 cm. If the system is supplied energy at the rate of 2 kW, the time taken to move one of the charges to the mid point of the line joining the other two charges is
explanation:
According to the question,

Energy of system of three charges,

\( U = \left( \frac{kq_1 q_2}{r} \right)^3 \)

or \( U = \frac{k(2 \times 10^{-3} \times 2 \times 10^{-3})}{(1/2)} \times 3 = 24 k \times 10^{-6} \text{J} \)

When one charge move to the mid point of the line joining the other two charges.

Energy of the system,

\( U' = \frac{k(2 \times 2 \times 10^{-6})}{(1/4)} \times 2 + \frac{k(2 \times 2 \times 10^{-6})}{1/2} \)

\( U' = 40 k \times 10^{-6} \text{J} \)

Net energy, \( \Delta U = U' - U = (40 - 24) k \times 10^{-6} \)

or \( \Delta U = W = 16 \times 9 \times 10^{-3} \text{J} \)

Given that energy supplied to the system at the rate of 2 kW.

So, \( W = 2 \text{kW} \times t = 16 \times 9 \times 10^{-3} = 2 \times 10^{-2} \times t \)

\( \Rightarrow t = 72 \text{s} \).
name: Questions Item 23

text: A regular hexagon of side 5 cm has a charge 10 μC at each of its vertices. The potential at the centre of hexagon is
explanation:
Given,

length of sides of hexagon, \( r = 5 \text{cm} = 5 \times 10^{-2} \text{m} \)

charge on each vertices of hexagon, \( q = 10 \mu\text{C} = 10 \times 10^{-6} \text{C} \)

Now, according to the question,

Due to one charge, potential on hexagon's centre P is given as, potential, \( V = \frac{1}{4\pi\epsilon_0} \frac{q}{r} \)

Potential, \( V = \frac{kq}{r} \)

\( \left[ \because k = \frac{1}{4\pi\epsilon_0}, k = 9 \times 10^9 \text{C}^2 / \text{N-m}^2 \right] \)

\( V = \frac{9 \times 10^9 \times 10 \times 10^{-6}}{5 \times 10^{-2}} = 9 \times 2 \times 10^3 \times 10^2 \)

\( V = 18 \times 10^5 \text{N-m}^2 / \text{C}^2 \)

Due to all six charges, total potential at the centre of hexagonal is \( V_{\text{total}} = 6 \times (V_i) \)

Now, from Eq. (i), we get

\( V_{\text{total}} = 6 \times 18 \times 10^5 \Rightarrow V_{\text{total}} = 108 \times 10^7 \text{V} \).
name: Questions Item 24

text: The potential in an electric field varies as \( V = (x^2 - y^2) \). The electric lines of the force in X-Y plane are
explanation:
The potential in an electric field varies as,

\( \therefore \text{Electric field, } E = - \left[ \frac{dV_x}{dx} + \frac{dV_y}{dy} \right] \)

\( [\because E = \Delta V] \)

\( = - \left[ \frac{d}{dx}(x^2 - y^2)\hat{i} + \frac{d}{dy}(x^2 - y^2)\hat{j} \right] \)

\( E = -[2x\hat{i} - 2y\hat{j}] \Rightarrow E = -2x\hat{i} + 2y\hat{j} \)

Expression of the electric field is linear equation in two variables, i.e. straight lines in X-Y plane and slope having 45°, 35° etc.
name: Questions Item 25
